data.title: Nebius: Overvaluation Miss Becomes A Win (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I am happy that I do not have the bad habit of selling my biggest winners no matter what. Yes, I missed the latest Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) rally due to my cautiousness in the previous thesis. However, the stock is extremely volatile and could move in the opposite direction as well, as it reacts to headlines quite nervously. Nevertheless, here we are in late September 2024 and Nebius already has a multi-billion deal with Microsoft in its pocket. I think this is truly a game-changer because having a deal with Microsoft elevates your reputation to another level and opens wide opportunities for new potential collaborations. I think the valuation remains a risk, but now there is much stronger conviction that Nebius can deliver big positive surprises. Therefore, I am inclined to continue buying while upgrading NBIS to a 'Strong Buy'.

Fundamental analysis

Wall Street analysts expect revenue of Nebius to grow exponentially within a couple of years, which is an extremely bullish sign. As a result, the company's P/S ratio is expected to drop to quite healthy levels of between 3x and 4x.

Such an aggressive revenue growth trajectory projected by Wall Street does not look unrealistic given the company's recent $17.4 billion AI infrastructure deal with Microsoft. This is a massive positive fundamental development in several ways.

First, the deal itself is massive and means billions in sales generated in the foreseeable future. This factor alone adds significant optimism because the deal amount is vast for a company of Nebius's scale. But it is just the beginning of positive factors.

In addition, having Microsoft among your customers might be worth even more than the money. Microsoft has a reputation as one of the world's most technologically advanced companies. Trusting Nebius's AI infrastructure to power Microsoft's new prospective offerings is a big quality sign underscoring the quality and reliability of Nebius and its infrastructure. Therefore, this deal might not be the last one for Nebius, and the company might be able to win new multi-billion dollar contracts with other giants like AWS or Google.

Second, the multi-billion dollar contract with a giant like Microsoft also highly likely decreases credit risks of Nebius in the eyes of lenders. It is one thing when you are just Nebius, a promising AI startup. And it is completely different when you have a massive contract with one of the world's largest corporations. Nebius's balance sheet is already quite strong, but this contract will highly likely open new large opportunities to raise debt financing, which is vital because AI infrastructure buildout isn't cheap.

The company is also working hard to improve its image among young, bright real-world AI and robotics startups and teams, which is vital if you want to recruit or partner with the best people who will bring their synergies. Together with Nvidia, Nebius has announced the introduction of the Nebius Robotics and Physical AI Awards, aiming to showcase companies advancing the frontier of physical AI. This development looks very promising from a long-term perspective, considering the explosive potential of physical AI.

Fresh information from the company that underwriters exercised full option to purchase additional Class A shares also looks very bullish. First of all, there was an underwriting "dream-team", including Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, BofA, and Citigroup. I think that these whales know very well what they do, and their participation in the underwriting means a lot in terms of institutional trust. The fact that full option was exercised also indicates strong investor appetite. Last but not least, this means a massive $4.3 billion cash raise, which will be invested in future growth.

With all these positive factors, it seems very reasonable that Wedbush recently specifically named Nebius as one of the "Best AI infrastructure names to play the theme." I would also like to draw readers' attention to the fact that Nebius was included in very "elite company" alongside the largest AI darlings like Nvidia, Microsoft, and Palantir. The fact that a major Wall Street firm told investors that Nebius is one of the best plays for the AI infrastructure boom is a significant factor that will highly likely attract more attention to the stock.

All these large positive factors, especially recognition from Wedbush and institutional investors, will highly likely be a solid fuel to support already strong share price momentum. As suggested above, the momentum is absolutely on fire, which certainly adds a lot of optimism to investors and attracts new ones.

Mitigating factors

Market cap of Nebius is currently $26.6 billion, which is more than 100 times larger than its TTM revenue. Of course, there is great potential to drive exponential revenue growth over the next few years, but what if they underperform compared to aggressive expectations? I mean, Nebius really has great potential, and that is why I am still holding the stock and plan to expand my position. But we have to be realistic and understand that no one guarantees these bold growth targets will be achieved — there is no 100% guarantee in the stock market. And in the case of Nebius, the probability of delivering growth is high, in my opinion, but not as high compared to other AI plays like Nvidia or other highly profitable giants.

My philosophy is simple and some people might think it's too simple, but this approach very rarely lets me down — if the EPS is growing, the share price will inevitably follow. And the main point making me cautious about the valuation of NBIS is that despite its expected exponential revenue growth over the next three years, its EPS is expected to remain negative. And it is highly uncertain what scale they will need to achieve before they become bottom-line profitable.

I want my readers to understand that Nebius is an extremely risky investment because the company is still not profitable, and the timing of breaking even looks extremely uncertain. I am investing because I do not want to miss out on such massive revenue growth; however, the level of uncertainty regarding operating leverage is extremely high.

Conclusion

I want to capitalize on the expected exponential revenue growth of Nebius, which is soaring. Valuation remains the big area of concern, but I think it is more likely that operating leverage is there than not. However, my confidence is not based on a proven track record of success from Nebius, making it far from a 100% probability. A classic high risk-high-reward play deserving a 'Strong Buy' rating.

data.title: Lithium Americas Gets The Trump Premium: Is The 70% Rally Justified?
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) is on the way to becoming the largest lithium miner in the country, with access to one of the largest lithium deposits in the world.

Lithium is a key element needed for batteries, a market that is growing rapidly with the advent of renewable energies and electric vehicles.

The stock just rallied 70% following rumours that the current administration was looking to acquire a stake in the company.

While not confirmed yet, this would be very much in character for President Donald Trump, who has been on somewhat of a buying spree, acquiring 10% of Intel (INTC) just recently.

For a stock that has often traded on speculation around lithium prices and permitting milestones, this kind of politically charged catalyst adds an entirely new dimension: the “Trump Premium.”

But what are the deeper implications of this news? Should investors be looking to buy after the rally?

Let’s dive right in.

The Rumour That Sparked A Rally

According to Reuters, the U.S. government, under Trump’s direction, is in talks to renegotiate a $2.26 billion Department of Energy loan previously awarded to Lithium Americas for its Thacker Pass project in Nevada. As part of the renegotiation, the administration may take up to a 10% equity stake in the company.

The loan from the DoE will be integral to Lithium America’s plan to begin mining at Thacker Pass, which is the largest known lithium mine in the U.S. and one of the largest in the world.

It’s also important to note that General Motors (GM) is also an important player here, having already agreed to invest $650 million for a significant equity position and offtake rights.

While Lithium Americas was always a company dependent on the government, through the DoE loan, an equity stake changes the fundamental relationship and probably improves the future prospects of LAC.

That’s at least what the market seems to believe.

The “Trump Premium” Explained

Markets often assign premiums when new catalysts change the perceived trajectory of a company. In this case, the Trump Premium can be thought of in three ways:

Political Cover

If Trump is tied to Lithium Americas, it’s hard to imagine federal agencies stalling permits or regulators adding friction. Political risk shifts from headwind to tailwind.

Strategic Signalling

Already, we’ve seen disputes between China and the U.S. over rare earth minerals. Clearly, lithium has become a strategic asset, and Trump just validated this by potentially taking a stake in LAC, which is bullish for Lithium and LAC.

Investor Sentiment

The simple association with Trump is enough to create a sense of security in investors.

But premiums can vanish as quickly as they appear. Which brings us to the fundamentals.

Fundamentals Still Matter

Lithium Americas has always been a high-beta play on the future of energy. The company doesn’t yet produce lithium, and therefore its value rests on successfully developing Thacker Pass.

This will, first of all, require high amounts of capital and external financing.

Without new capital, project execution is at risk. A government stake does reduce this concern, but it won’t eliminate cost inflation or delays.

Even with fast-tracking, large lithium mines take years to build. Initial production isn’t expected until late this decade. Near-term revenues remain limited.

The other issue is that Lithium prices have been volatile, spiking on EV adoption trends and then cooling on oversupply concerns.

Even if Thacker Pass comes online, profitability depends on where lithium prices settle.

The 70% Rally: Sustainable or Speculative?

The 70% move we’re seeing following this news may just be reflexive, with algos coming in and traders covering short positions.

Nonetheless, I do believe there’s room here for LAC to keep rallying.

With Trump potentially securing a 10% government stake, DOE financing is confirmed, and Lithium Americas implicitly has the support of the whole government, which may not even be limited to the White House.

Just like with Intel, I wouldn’t be surprised to now see other big companies increasing their stake or investments in LAC.

When Trump bought Intel, Nvidia (NVDA) stepped in with a $5 billion investment. Now with LAC, we can see General Motors (GM) or perhaps another large car company double down on their investments.

Lithium miners are notoriously difficult to value, as their worth depends on future commodity prices and project execution.

For the time being, LAC doesn’t even have revenues.

If they execute as expected, then we could see this rising to over half a billion by 2030, but this is honestly quite hard to predict.

LAC trades on a narrative right now, but I do think it’s a strong one.

Ultimately, if you believe lithium batteries will become commonplace in cars and grid systems, then LAC seems like a no-brainer.

Final Thoughts

Lithium Americas just became one of the most politically charged trades in the market.

A Trump stake, if confirmed, would cement LAC as a symbol of America’s push to secure critical minerals. That could mean years of government support and preferential treatment.

But it doesn't necessarily guarantee profitability or investor returns. If the priority is to make lithium for America, then investor interests could fall second.

All in all, this is a speculative play which I’m not chasing, though I am bullish on Lithium Americas given its strategic importance and government backing.

data.title: Palladyne AI: Targets The Next Industrial Revolution, Robotics AI
data.description:
Palladyne AI (NASDAQ:PDYN) is a highly speculative buy for high-risk tolerance profiles. My PDYN thesis is based on Palladyne AI’s strategic target on multi-hundred-billion-dollar AI software and industrial robotics markets. It has a debt-free balance sheet with over $62 million in cash. Palladyne holds a 2.5+ year operational runway to execute its product roadmap directly in line with big tailwinds in industrial automation and high U.S. government defense spending shifts.

Consensus topline estimates project an inflection point in FY2027 (+226%) and a clear EPS profitability by FY2029. These elements are supporting my mid-to-long-term valuation upsides. These levels are in line with short-term technical projections reflecting possible sooner than expected re-pricing (due to market efficiency).

To my bullish arguments, the primary risks include extreme forward valuation (P/S > 60x) that is disconnected from near-term fundamentals, that include projected revenue declines in 2025 and deepening losses in 2026. Further, the company’s developing commercial software experience and a lengthy 12-18 month sales cycle breed execution risks. This thesis considers PDYN stock a possible long-term investment in disruptive tech, but bulls may need high patience against near-term volatility. Therefore, the investment is not suitable for every investor profile.

Palladyne AI Targets Explosive Growth in Robotics and AI Services Markets

Palladyne AI is at a cross-section of several forward technological and geopolitical shifts. It has a fortified balance sheet with a clear product roadmap targeting vast-growth markets. Palladyne's forward topline growth is rooted in the huge scale of the target market and progressive capabilities of its AI software. I have analyzed specific macro and industry outlook that solidifies the updraft for Palladyne AI stock valuation. For instance, the robotics tech market may expand from $108.55 billion in 2025 to $375.95 billion by 2034 with a CAGR of 46.1%. This high rate of market expansion creates a massive addressable market for Palladyne's main technologies. To be specific, Palladyne AI is targeting automation, which is the next frontier of AI. For instance, Palladyne’s AI scales autonomous systems to be capable of perceiving, reasoning, and acting independently. This next wave of AI can possibly derive $450 billion-$650 billion in additional annual revenue in advanced industries by 2030.

Logically, I see Palladyne's mission to enable robots and drones to "think like humans," moving beyond simple pre-programmed automation to handle dynamic and complex tasks, a big deal for business growth. This opportunity frames Palladyne's software as a base component of the "third phase of AI evolution," which can be seen as a "services as a software" market with a $3 trillion to $5 trillion direct opportunity. This third wave is like AI-powered software platforms delivering traditional business services. This is a direct description of Palladyne's value proposition, considering its Palladyne IQ and Palladyne Pilot products.

Palladyne's mid-2025 business update outlines its strategy to capitalize on "Manufacturing Reinvention," "Public Safety Modernization," and "Government & Defense AI Spending." These specific trends represent major industrial shifts in progress. Here, President Trump's reshoring agenda and defense-focused stimulus create a big domestic market for AI-based robotics to offset labor shortages and boost the US’s industrial competitiveness against huge labor markets like India, China, and Southeast Asia. This applied technology’s use is visible in increased annual budget allocations from U.S. government agencies like DoD that reached a total of $338 billion in FY2024. The FY2025 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) marks over $950 billion for defense spending, with a large share for missile defense, counter-drone systems, and AI/autonomous systems [the exact applications targeted by Palladyne Pilot]. I am not saying that there is no competition for these shifts, but my argument is based on Palladyne pipeline’s direct alignment with government spending priorities that can create a well-funded and receptive client base and de-risk a majority of its market entry strategy.

PDYN Stock Price Growth with Strong Financials and Bullish Revenue Projections

Financially, Palladyne AI has solidified its position to execute the strategy. As of Q2-FY2025, it has a solid balance sheet with $62.7 million in cash and has no long-term debt. [Although it has Y/Y reduced Warrant liabilities and Operating lease.] This is a big advantage for an early-stage tech firm that allows it to invest in growth without the burden of interest payments. This financial health is not accidental. Palladyne raised $34.8 million net of expenses in H1-FY2025 through at-the-market offerings and the exercise of warrants. This kind of capital raising in the current market environment indicates strong market confidence in Palladyne stock’s long-term potential. Further, Palladyne has a stated cash burn of $2 million per month against its cash reserves, which provides a runway of over 2.5 years (assuming no new revenues). This mid-term runway allows Palladyne to endure the 12-to-18-month sales cycles it anticipates without facing a liquidity crisis.

The forward-estimates and technical analysis also support stock price upside targets. Consensus revenue estimates for FY2025 project a Y/Y decline due to the wind-down of legacy hardware products, but the projections for next year hold progressive growth. Analysts forecast Palladyne’s topline to grow 44.23% in FY2026 and that is followed by a big 226.90% in FY2027, and another Y/Y topline jump of 63.21% in FY2028. This "J-curve" revenue profile is the mark of a tech company transitioning to a high-margin recurring software-as-a-service model. Similarly, consensus EPS estimates project the company will reach green profitability in FY2029 with EPS growing an estimated 342.86% Y/Y in FY2030. For me, these projections indicate that the current CapEx and cash burn may lead to big profitability.

The technical roadmap also supports my argument of a bullish outlook. Following Fibonacci extensions based on the stock's weekly price movements projects decisively bullish price targets. The 0.618 extension level marks a price target of $15.6, and the more aggressive 1.618 extension projects a target of $21 in the next 12 months. These levels represent a potential upside of 97% and 166% from the stock price of $7.9 (as of September 21, 2025, at the time of writing). A breakout in the stock price can turn into a continued bullish momentum if it stably closes above the modified quarterly exponential moving average (blended green-white lines) that is in line with a 0.786 retracement. These technical targets are more aggressive than Wall Street's consensus average price target of $11.5, which indicates a 45.20% price upside in the next 1-year. To answer why? My technical assessment follows the short-term volatility in the stock price and mostly focuses on the recent price swings. Looking forward, the immediate stock buy position must be averaged down in the range of $6.8-$5.35 till $3.47 (1.272 between 1.618 downside extensions) to take advantage of the stock’s volatile price movements (PDYN has a 60-month Beta of 3.75).

On performing the theoretical valuation, I applied industry-standard valuation multiples to the consensus topline projections to derive long-term price targets. As there is a lack of profitability until FY2029, a forward Price-to-Sales (P/S) multiple is the most appropriate (what I see) base valuation metric. But I used the Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple for the final year. PDYN’s forward P/S is 62.51x, that is extremely high compared to the sector median of 1.69x. This means that the market is pricing in huge forward growth. Projected FY2030 revenue is $66.25 million, and the assigned FY2030 P/S multiple is between 10x and 15x, as I assume Palladyne to be a mature high-growth high-margin AI software company that can command a high P/S multiple normalized towards the sector median. This is a big normalization from the current 60x+ multiple with a solid growth and margin profile (as I am being too conservative here because assumed multiples are well below the valuation data for AI comps).

With all that, the projected FY2030 market cap range is between (10x P/S: $66.25 million x 10) $662.5 million and (15x P/S: $66.25 million x 15) $993.75 million. The range projects a market cap upside of 99.9%-199.86% relative to the current level ($331.4 million) by FY2030. Similarly, using forward P/E valuation based on projected FY2030 EPS of $0.31 and assigned P/E multiple of Given the projected 342% EPS growth in 2030 and continued market expansion, a P/E ratio of ~60x (near average forward PE value of top-10 tech leaders) is reasonable as a lead comp in a high-AI-tech sector. It provides a projected 2030 share price of $0.31 x 60 = $18.6 per share (135% upside from the current stock price).

High Valuation Faces Risks of Sharp Decline Amid Persistent Losses and Uncertain Growth

Now, on the risks, the main risk lies in Palladyne's extreme valuation amid a lack of current profitability. There is a severe disconnect between its market capitalization and its financials. The P/S (TTM) ratio stands at 56.49x, that is 3,234% higher than the industrial sector median of 1.69. Similarly, the Forward P/S ratio is 62.51 with a 3,603% premium to the sector. These topline valuation metrics indicate that the stock is priced for a level of forward growth that is far from guaranteed and leaves no room for operational misses. This valuation is not supported by current health, as Palladyne's Return on Common Equity (TTM) is a highly negative -107.11%, its Levered FCF Margin is -293.18%, and its Net Income Per Employee is a loss of over $630K. These metrics indicate a business that is aggressively burning through capital. Q2-FY2025 had a loss from ops of $15 million for H1-FY2025 and a massive accumulated deficit of $475.6 million. The history of losses, as stated in the company's own risk factors (in SEC filings), may continue "for the foreseeable future," making the current valuation appear problematic instead of potent.

More on this, the consensus EPS estimate for FY2026 marks a decline of 3,350% to -$0.69 from an estimated -$0.02 in FY2025. This suggests that as Palladyne attempts to scale its commercial ops, expenses may balloon far faster than revenues. This can lead to an acceleration in cash burn and a deeper bottomline. This projected deterioration may necessitate more dilutive financing rounds well before the company reaches the profitable years of FY2029-2030. This is bad for the stock’s price as these factors erode the value for current stock holders. The history of earnings surprises also raises concerns as Palladyne has missed EPS estimates by -168%, -47%, -70%, and -85% in the last four FYs. This indicates a pattern of bottomline underperformance relative to expectations.

Finally, the performance of the stock also indicates extreme volatility and potential downside. A high of 652% total return in 1 year (346% as of now) can be misleading, as the stock's 3-year total return is -47%. These return swings show PDYN’s capacity for massive price destruction and creation. This level of volatility is a risk as a price break below the current price channel, the stock can fall to the next support levels at $5.35 or even $3.47. To be clearer on risk, a failure to meet the optimistic revenue growth forecasts for FY2026 and beyond can trigger a large sell-off, and the market can re-price the stock through lower lows based on delayed profitability and heightened execution problems.

___To sum up, the current investment case for Palladyne AI is a high-risk, high-reward move, totally focused on its potential to become a foundational software provider for the next-gen of intelligent automations. The presence of a massive addressable market in agentic AI for robotics, a long-term debt-free balance sheet with a multi-year cash runway, and a clear strategic alignment with major government and industrial digitization trends tilted my analytical conclusion for a buy (Speculative Buy!) for those with the capacity to absorb very high volatility (to be very clear, the potential for maximum capital loss).

The recommended approach is to initiate a small long-position at current levels and employ a dollar-cost averaging (DCA) strategy over the next 12-18 months. Target near-term price declines from projected revenue troughs and widening losses, and methodically building a position to capture the anticipated growth inflection in FY2026-2027. The primary accumulation zone should be within the Fibonacci retracement levels of $5.35 to $7.98, then $3.47.

To validate/invalidate my PDYN stock thesis over the next 6-24 months, a solid monitoring of the following specific metrics is needed: Commercial Traction and Revenue Recognition [the announcement of the major commercial contracts for Palladyne IQ/Pilot and monitor press releases and SEC filings for these announcements throughout H2-FY2025 and H1-FY2026], Revenue Ramp vs. Estimates [Track quarterly revenue against analyst forecasts and a failure to show a clear ramp by mid-2026 can be a major red flag], Operational and Runway Integrity [ scrutinize the Net cash used in operating activities and verify monthly burn as not materially increased], Gross Profit Margin with consensus revisions in profitability projections, Government/Defense Pipeline [Monitor company updates for progress on defense-related contracts tied to the FY2025 NDAA, mainly in counter-drone, ISR, and autonomy initiatives] and the emergence of a superior commercial agentic AI platform that directly marginalizes Palladyne's core "on-the-edge" value proposition].

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Look At The Quantum Computing Sector, With IonQ At The Center Of The Debate
data.description:
Important note: This piece was written as both a full quantum computer industry breakdown and a look at IonQ, Inc. (NYSE:IONQ). The Sell rating relates solely to IonQ. You could skip any of those and go straight to what interests you. I find the industry part far more fascinating. Thanks for reading!

Introduction

Understanding quantum computing as a sector is a challenging task, especially for us, as investors. We are not physicists, and you would prefer me to talk about the sector from an investing standpoint: what is the size of the market, when is the best time to invest, and how much money you could make. To answer all of these questions without speculation, we would need this industry to be fully operating for a couple of years. Then, you would get extensive research, like the one that I did with the semiconductor industry. But quantum computing is still very much an idea and research, rather than a reality.

Today, I have challenged myself with a breakdown of this industry, and this article may be demanding of you as a reader as well. Since the sector is fresh and not yet developed, we will need to try and understand how it works, what tasks a quantum computer could solve, who would pay for it, and finally, how big the opportunity is for investors. If it’s not the first article of mine that you read, you know I always try to explain everything as plain as possible. Hopefully, today will be no different, and I’ll succeed.

The interest in quantum computing has spiked significantly after the Memorandum of Understanding between the U.S. and the U.K. took place last week. President Trump named quantum computing one of the top issues to collaborate on, alongside artificial intelligence and nuclear energy. I just covered the latter in my Oklo (OKLO) breakdown. Trump's words sent the whole industry flying up, and everyone seems to be talking about it till today.

Understanding Quantum Computing

Let’s jump right into the explanation of what quantum computing is, and I promise it will be worth your while, as if you will really understand it, you will be able to create your personal opinion on the sector, without hoping analysts get their predictions right.

The broad idea behind quantum computers is that they will be able to process or “check” multiple large numbers simultaneously. Sounds unimpressive at first, but it’s far more nuanced than that. Let’s take, for example, encryption, and let’s imagine we want to decrypt it.

Regular computers perform using bits 0 and 1. Every code, every action your computers make behind the scenes is coded in nulls and ones. Encryption is the same. It’s a large amount of nulls and ones that are impossible to decrypt as of this moment. For example, now we use 1024-bit encryption, meaning you have a binary number made up of 1024 zeroes or ones. If you don’t know what the exact number is, you end up with 2 to the power of 1024 different combinations for this encryption, which is an enormous number (about 309 digits). To decrypt it, your computer would need to try every possible combination. If we were to take all of the computers in the world (about 10 billion of them), and make them try out all of the combinations at a fair speed, modern computers work (which is already very fast), we would end up waiting so long, the age of our universe would be a blink in comparison. So, it is practically impossible.

Here is where quantum computers get interesting, because they perform using qubits, which could be both zero and one, but also could be anything in between. And it opens a whole world of different possibilities. Besides, there is a Shor’s algorithm that was designed to factor large numbers in polynomial time (fast enough to be useful). It means that a big enough quantum computer could break 1024-bit encryption in hours or days, practically replacing 10 billion machines that would need to work few eternities to achieve the same results. Sounds scary, fascinating, depends on how you look at it, but there is a major problem. We have no idea if we (as a humanity) will ever be able to build a quantum computer large enough, and now we get to the most important part for us as for investors: understanding what we buy when we invest in the sector.

To understand to scale of the problem, we will need to go deeper into qubits. When a qubit becomes 0 or 1, it stops being a qubit and becomes a bit, like in regular computing, and it’s called decoherence. Putting it simply, imagine a spinning coin. In this spinning state, it’s both heads and tails until it stops spinning. A qubit, the same as a coin, can’t be in this state for a long time, and many exterior impacts can decohere it. For example, heat, radiation, magnetic field, vibrations, and other tiniest things we are not paying attention to are making a qubit “fall,” and it becomes a bit at 1 or 0. A quantum computer will work only if those qubits stay in this “both at once” state. If they don’t stay long enough in that state, they won’t be able to finish the computation before collapsing, and this is the reason why they are held frozen in very special conditions where almost nothing can disturb them. Even in those almost perfect conditions, they decohere, because this is what they do.

Now, you can already understand how unstable and complicated the whole story is. The computer will work only if the qubits stay long in a state, in which they do not stay long due to their properties.

Then, an error-detection process started, and we got logical qubits as a result. Those are artificially created qubits that are being kept at this fragile state much longer than the physical qubits, and the error rates are way lower. To create it, you would need to take a lot of regular qubits, often more than a thousand, and encode one logical qubit's information across all of them. Most of the qubits are simply checking for errors in other qubits. If you actually check in what state the qubit currently is, you would destroy it, so they check patterns (syndromes) that will explain whether the qubit is still a qubit. I won’t go into detail here, just trust me when I say the process is very difficult, and the structure of it resembles a very shaky building that could collapse literally any second. Just to get one logical qubit. A real quantum computer, like the one we discussed before when I gave an encryption example, would need thousands of logical qubits working together. If each logical qubit takes 1,000 physical qubits, and a big algorithm needs 1,000 logical qubits, that’s a million physical qubits that need to work well.

Some companies showed significant progress in this. For example, IBM (IBM) reached a correction code 10x more efficient than the previous one, using only 288 physical qubits in a logical qubit. There are some trial runs from Microsoft (MSFT) and Quantinuum that potentially could turn 30 qubits into 4 logical qubits, which makes this imaginary structure of mine less shaky, but it's still far-far away from making some real progress.

How Close Are We To The Quantum Computer?

Going back to our encryption, for a quantum computer to be powerful to be able to crack 1024-bit encryption, you’d need approximately 2,000 logical qubits. If we take that each of them requires about 200 physical qubits, that’s 400,000 fragile qubits working well together. Alphabet's (GOOG) quantum computing processor, which made a lot of noise in 2019, was a 54-qubit processor. Not 54 logical qubits. 54 physical qubits. IBM's biggest quantum processor has 1000 physical qubits. The plan is to scale that to 100,000 qubits by 2033 (we do not yet know that it’s even possible to scale), but even the company itself points out that the roadmap beyond that is unclear due to many factors, one of them being cost-effectiveness.

Regular computing isn’t at a steady state. It progresses as time goes by. It could happen that by the time someone achieves 400,000 qubits, we would really need three times that to decode the next generation encryption that will be beyond 2048 bits. And don’t get me wrong, it’s not that someone needs to work hard enough to really “get” to this point. It may prove to be impossible to make a processor of so many qubits, unless we make some serious technological progress that will change the game entirely.

Now, who could be the companies that could make any kind of breakthroughs? Most likely, it’s huge names like IBM, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Google, Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft. Companies that have the resources to work on this and, most importantly, pay for it. It’s an incredibly complex process that can be simplified with money, and those who have the most of it will be able to build the right infrastructure, hire the much-needed talent, and cover and absorb those enormous costs. Besides, governments could be able to make a breakthrough in financing different companies and universities. For now, it looks like the most interested ones in this race are the U.S., the U.K., China, Israel, and the EU.

But what about the “pure” quantum plays, you will ask. And I will talk about those, if I must, but I have to warn you - with everything I have learned from making this extensive research, I think most of these popular pure quantum names could fall apart sooner than quantum computing has a chance to become something real. Does this mean there is no investing opportunity? Both yes and no. I don’t see a real investing opportunity beyond big companies and the names that are being backed by the government. But there is a trading opportunity.

One of the “pure” companies I noticed is Quantinuum, which is private but is being backed by Honeywell (HON). They proved that logical qubits can outperform physical qubits. Basically, what I discussed before is based on their progress. They’ve entangled 50 logical qubits and their accuracy at above 98%. This is an absolute record in the field. Of course, 50 logical qubits are not enough to build that quantum computer everyone imagines, but their results are still very impressive.

I wrote an article about Honeywell back in April and gave it a Hold rating, but I did not address their quantum side. Now that they are one of the companies to partner with the Department of Energy on quantum usage in space, it could be interesting to take another look at them, and I think I will do it in a separate piece.

This time, I will break down a company that is a quantum player on its own, the most popular one, IonQ.

IonQ’s Technology

Remember the explanation of how fragile those qubits are? Companies try to bundle them in logical qubits to make them “stronger.” IonQ works a bit differently. They are not using superconducting circuits, like all of the other major players. Instead, their physical qubits are trapped ions. The idea is that trapped ions in a vacuum are shielded better, and they can keep their quantum state longer. In this case, seconds, compared to microseconds. At the same time, they are slower to move because they are being manipulated using lasers, but they are more precise and stable at the same time. So while IBM and Google are trying to make many superconducting circuits work together and survive long enough to build logical qubits, IonQ is trying to prove that if each physical qubit is stronger by design, they won’t need so many levels of protection and so difficult bundles. In theory, it could mean that they will need far fewer regular qubits to make a logical qubit.

It’s important to understand, when investing in standalone names, that companies like IonQ live or die by quantum. There won’t be a business beyond it. So while it’s a pure quantum play, they could easily shut down if things go tough.

The Financial Reality

In the latest quarter, IonQ reported $20.7 million in revenue, which was significantly up from $7.6 million in March. They beat the expectations by ~$3.5 million, which means the demand for their research and development is growing. On the other hand, the company lost over $160 million in the same quarter, with R&D spending being over $100 million and SG&A expenses getting close to $60 million.

This company burns an insane amount of money every quarter (all of them do, to be fair). Net income for the quarter ending in June was negative $177 million. IonQ is nowhere near profitability, and they do not even talk about it yet. It could be years or even more than a decade for this company to become operating and profitable, if it is still around until then.

Its enterprise value-to-sales multiple is over 200x, looking forward, which is almost 7000% overvalued compared to the sector median. It means investors are valuing this company like some insanely trending biotech. According to this valuation, the market is sure that IonQ will make a world-changing breakthrough and become the name of the quantum future.

The only positive thing in the financials is the balance sheet. The company holds more than half a billion dollars in cash and has almost no debt, which could allow it to keep burning money for more than a year with no extra funding, which is already something. It’s not on the verge of bankruptcy, which is the most you can ask from a company like this.

That cushion has been created by the stock dilution, by the way. Shares outstanding have climbed from about 200 million at the end of 2022 to about 300 million today. For existing shareholders, that has been painful, but on the other hand, the company keeps itself alive, which is good. More dilutions are expected in the future when the cash position starts to decrease.

Recent Progress

Over the past year, IonQ has slowly become a commercial player. The company has signed a $54 million contract with the Air Force Research Lab. It has collaborations with AstraZeneca (AZN) for drug discovery and with Nvidia (NVDA) and Amazon (AMZN) for other purposes. In September, IonQ partnered with Honeywell and the Department of Energy. A few days later, it revealed a breakthrough in photon conversion, something they worked on with the Air Force.

The Investment Case

IonQ has a $22 billion market cap. It’s valued as if it has already become the dominant quantum player. Forward revenue estimates are under $100 million for 2025, which means the company trades at over 200 times sales. This multiple is much higher than Nvidia was in the middle of AI euphoria. It’s much higher than Palantir (PLTR), with a billion in revenue in a single quarter from AI.

The justification for that valuation lies entirely in the future. Now, how long would it take to justify it even if everything goes the best way possible? It’s really hard to say. It could be a few years if they make some breakthrough at the end, or it could really be decades if things stall.

If IonQ really can scale trapped ions to tens of thousands of logical qubits by the early 2030s (after the research, it sounds impossible to me), its systems could become the most advanced and wanted in the world. If, however, scaling proves slower than promised (I can easily see why that would happen), or superconducting circuits beat the trapped-ion model to it, the current valuation could collapse and take with it all of your money.

Conclusion

I see this very simply - big names have the resources to make a breakthrough, and not to go bankrupt on the way. Smaller companies, most likely, will break down under their own promises. IonQ has some revenue, and it’s a green flag. It won’t go bankrupt soon. Beyond this, it’s a gamble. An exciting one for sure, the one you can definitely make if you feel like it, but still a gamble. Can the stock fly higher? Definitely, it can always go higher if the demand is high. Once again, I am so very skeptical. The market is unreasonable, but still, I don’t get who those people are that pay so a huge price for something that can turn out to be nothing.

The quantum space is fascinating. It’s very interesting. I did not include so much information I gathered to make this piece as clear and easy to understand as possible, but I was digging for days. When I started the research, I already knew quite a bit from before. In general, I am happy with the stocks I hold, and I will take a look once again at Honeywell and probably IBM. I won’t be buying any of the pure small quantum names, because I know how much it costs to make these developments. I think the chances are huge that tiny names will go bankrupt before they make any breakthroughs. Of course, the stock could fly on some news about Trump, or whatever other noise. I have no way of predicting it, and I don’t want to play fortune teller.

data.title: Xpel May Finally Be At An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Xpel (NASDAQ:XPEL) provides premium films and coatings for cars, mostly for painted surfaces but also for windows. These coatings, while generally transparent, can offer protection to maintain the finish of a car. These are often an add-on for those purchasing premium vehicles and can cost thousands of dollars. The cost of Xpel is expensive in absolute terms, roughly $6,000 for full car coverage or $1,000 for windows. However, it can be a relatively trivial cost for those purchasing a premium sports car and not wanting to spend time regularly cleaning and detailing it.

Historically this has been a good business. Xpel has maintained high margins and sustained a relatively attractive level of growth. That said, in recent years the stock hit levels that were arguably priced for perfection. It perhaps now trades at a more attractive valuation as the EV/EBITDA and price to earnings data below suggests. That should enable what appears to be a high-quality stock to outperform over time.

Car sales as an Important Metric

At a high level, the health of Xpel is tied to new car sales. For every new car (and especially each new premium car) that is sold, Xpel has an opportunity to attach its product. The seasonally adjusted annual rate (SAAR) of car sales in the U.S. can be tracked here. Now of course the relationship isn't quite that simple because Xpel typically has a higher attach rate to premium cars owned by enthusiasts.

U.S. car sales so far for 2025 have recovered from the volatile pandemic period, but remain below the highs of 2014-2019. Generally though, it's important to remember that Xpel's attach rate to vehicles is relatively low (offering some opportunity for growth), a cyclical slowdown in car purchases would still hurt growth, but to the extent attach rate of protective films and coatings on vehicles increases over time, Xpel has a long runway for growth.

Barriers To Entry

The fact that Xpel enjoys relatively high gross margins suggests barriers to entry. Xpel's gross margin has ranged from 39% to 42% over its past three fiscal years (2022-2024). According to NYU Stern's dataset of industry gross margins, that's high in absolute terms and significantly above the automotive average of 11% gross margins. In fact, Seeking Alpha's quant analysis groups the stock as consumer discretionary rather than automotive, which may be a better fit.

There are likely to be multiple reasons for Xpel's attractive margin structure. The first is the often cited proprietary DAP software which enables Xpel to provide precise film cutting measurements for a broad range of vehicles to its installers (over 80,000 vehicles according to the company's June 2025 investor presentation). That's a high cost investment that may be difficult and costly for an entrant to replicate.

However, it's likely that Xpel's competitive advantage stems from multiple factors beyond software alone. For example, Xpel has a strong reputation for quality, helped in part by distributor training they offer, and a global footprint. This is something that would take years to create from scratch. Xpel also has relationships with OEMs such as Rivian that are hard to displace as well as many global aftermarket relationships. Plus Xpel has a brand that resonates with car enthusiasts. For example, if you look across Reddit automotive message boards you will see consistent endorsement of Xpel, for example "works well, and it a top tier product" [sic] and "it all came out amazing", albeit with acknowledgement that is an expensive, premium product.

Also, the cost of Xpel's service is quite low compared to the value of the service performed. Xpel can be a small component of a luxury car purchase and yet help maintain the key aspect of a sports car, it's visual appeal for years. Yes, if you're a wealthy car purchaser, you could gamble on a cheaper option, but why take the risk when you might save 2% of the total purchase price of the car? And if you get it wrong, you might be spending extra hours washing, detailing or fixing your car when your time is expensive.

Quality and Innovation

For an equity investment to work the question is not what they are doing now, but what will they be doing five years from now and beyond. In that regard Xpel continues to innovate. Earlier this month Xpel launched color films. This means that rather than just protecting your car, you can change its color or finish. Obviously the market reception to this remains to be seen, but this demonstrates innovation and broadens the use cases for Xpel's products. Previously you could protect your car, but now you can materially alter its look.

Also, interestingly, whereas Xpel's coatings are a premium product, changing the color of your car with a colored film, may actually be more cost effective than getting your car repainted.

Long-Term Opportunities

Xpel may have opportunities beyond automotive over time. Currently, residential and marine usage of its products represent under 5% of sales and present challenges in terms of brand awareness and distribution models, but may be an additional source of growth for Xpel for the medium-term.

There is also an interesting marketing challenge for Xpel in that their product is basically invisible and if it's working well you literally don't see it. This makes viral marketing something of a challenge, this is something the CEO thinks about and if a solution can be found, Xpel may perform even better. The recent focus on colored films and tints may offer something of an opportunity here.

Alignment of Incentives

It should be noted that the CEO of Xpel holds 3.9% of the stock. Bonus payments for senior management is primarily calibrated on earnings per share, revenue growth and return on invested capital. The threshold for a revenue growth payout being met under the long term incentive plan is a 3-year average of 8% (that's for the minimum payout, other thresholds are higher). Generally, it appears management compensation and stock holdings are well aligned with shareholders. This information is as of the 2025 proxy.

Valuation

Importantly, Xpel achieves strong profitability across most metrics when compared to the consumer discretionary sector. It is good to see high profitability per employee with strong returns on capital, assets and equity. These metrics can drive a premium multiple if supported by growth.

The company has a net cash position currently. And it appears likely that the company can deliver $50M of free cash flow for 2025. The first six months of 2025, saw $28M of free cash flow, 2024 saw $41M.

Then we can see the following valuations at different free cash flow multiples (27.7M diluted shares):

Multiple (year end 2025) 20x 25x 30x
Implied share price $36 $45 $54

This suggests that the stock may be inexpensively valued today if growth can be sustained. For example, the iShares GARP ETF as a proxy for U.S. GARP valuations currently trades on a 40x historic P/E. The broader S&P 500 is at 31x earnings currently. It therefore appears Xpel is inexpensively valued compared to GARP stocks and the broader market.

Also the company's cash balance represents $1.80/share, I don't include that in the analysis as I doubt that will be returned to shareholders any time soon, but reinvested for growth. In fact, it may have already been spent, in part, on the Chinese distributor deal.

Catalysts

The main catalysts are for the company to deliver its historic performance without incident:

  • The company will need to show continued quarterly growth. In Q1 2024, albeit at a more demanding valuation the stock fell materially after reporting 5% growth. Q3 is expected in early November, Q4 in late February.
  • U.S. vehicle sales will need to remain positive, so far in 2025 that is occurring, but with tariff policy and other factors adding some month-to-month volatility.
  • The company will need to remain cautious with M&A. The terms of the recent Chinese acquisition are unclear.

Key Risks

Disintermediation by OEMs - it's possible the OEMs apply film or coatings to vehicles themselves, limiting the opportunity for after-market vendors such as Xpel. Tesla has already done this, however in the case of Rivian this has been done, but in partnership with Xpel.

Material decline in car sales - car sales are likely to slow in a recession, causing Xpel's growth to decline. For now car sales remain robust, but any decline particularly in premium car sales volumes would hurt Xpel.

Risks to reputation - Xpel enjoys a relatively strong reputation for quality currently, if that changes the long-term success of the business could be at risk.

Overpaying for M&A - Xpel has approximately $50M on the balance sheet as of June 2025, but recently acquired assets of its Chinese distributor, with detailed financial terms not disclosed. It is often hard to assess bolt-on acquisitions, but Xpel could overpay for deals in a way that hurts shareholders.

Technological innovation - to the extent automotive paints and coatings improve over time there may be less need to aftermarket coatings and films.

Summary

It appears that Xpel has consistently been a relatively attractive business, but due to apparent overvaluation has not always been an attractive stock.

Now that may have changed with the company still enjoying attractive margins, growth and capital return metrics but trading on under 20x estimated free cash flow for 2025. That's when peers might trade at 30x or 40x in the current market and when growth is running at 14% for the first six months of 2025. Provided Xpel can deliver robust results over the coming quarters, that may be a catalyst for this apparently high-quality stock to re-rate, which together with potential growth in underlying cash flow/earnings could provide an attractive setup for shareholders.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Google: Near-Term Might Be Stretched, But It Is Still A Long-Term Winner
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at one of the smaller positions in my portfolio, which only recently started to show some life, making me miss the boat on accumulating on the lows of the year.

Last time I covered Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) was back in August of last year, when I maintained my buy recommendation, saying that the dip was a godsend. Since then, the company’s share price has appreciated by over 51% against SPY’s (SPY) 20%.

I believe the company is valued close to or at its fair value, but that doesn’t mean it is not a good long-term investment in general, and long-term investors shouldn’t be afraid to add at such levels and accumulate if it drops down due to near-term profit-taking or other problems.

Briefly on Performance

Over the last year and a half, the company’s sales have been on a great trajectory. The company’s revenues have been increasing steadily. Honestly, I didn’t expect anything else from the juggernaut. The company’s services are still in high demand.

In terms of efficiency and profitability, we can see that operating and net margins have experienced a slight slowdown since the beginning of 2025. Is that an issue? If you didn’t look at the company in more detail, you may think that it is, but in reality, this was expected. The company is in one of the biggest spending sprees due to the AI race between the largest tech companies out there, and with that comes heavy investments in capex. Just recently, the company increased its capex outlook by another $10B, bringing the total to now $85B for 2025 as AI and cloud demand drive growth. This type of spending is unprecedented. Collectively, big tech firms are on their way to spend $400B on AI infrastructure and everything related. This is not going to last, and I expect margins to begin improving eventually.

Looking at the company’s financial position, we can see that it is still very strong, just as strong as it was a year ago. GOOG finished the quarter with $21B in cash and equivalents, as well as $74B in marketable securities, bringing its total liquidity to around $95B. It is worth noting, too is that the company’s debt more than doubled since the end of 2024, going from around $11B to around $23B, which for such a massive company like Alphabet, is nothing. GOOG’s EBIT for the quarter was over $31B, which can easily cover all the debt outstanding.

Overall, there is nothing that causes me concern right now. The company’s top line is growing at a respectable pace, around low teens, and the flattening of the margins is easily explainable and not concerning, given the intense competition of the AI race.

In the next sections, I will present some reasons that an investment in Alphabet is warranted and reasons that may deter some.

Reasons to Invest

Antitrust Case

I remember seeing that the company’s share price has been quite the laggard at the beginning of the year, compared to the rest of the big tech. One reason I believe that was the case is because of the regulatory issues it was going through up until now. Many investors try to stay away from such drama, which means the company’s shares will be trading at rather subdued valuations for a while. Just a quarter ago, the company was trading below a 20 P/E ratio, which was a blessing for the long-term investors. Just at the beginning of this month (September), the judges ruled in favor of Alphabet and allowed it to keep its Chrome browser, sending shares up almost 10% on the day. With the antitrust case out of the way, the investors will be able to go forward and support the company’s progress in the AI race. It seems that investors were expecting the antitrust case to go in favor of the company since the beginning of the summer, sending the company’s share on a massive rally.

Google Search Dominance

There is just nothing beating the pioneer in this segment. Everyone previously thought Google Search was going to be extinct, now that Co-Pilot has been introduced into Microsoft’s (MSFT) Bing. I have to say, I did use Bing at the beginning of last year, but it didn’t keep me around for long. Now that there is integration of GOOG’s Gemini, the search experience has evolved significantly, helping the company retain its dominance globally. Just look at the image below to see why there is absolutely no competition when it comes to Google.

Some might say it is dying only because they are not using GOOG’s, but that clearly remains far and few in between. I am not utilizing Google search or Gemini for full disclosure, as I am using Perplexity for my everyday needs, which would go into the “Other” category of the image above. There’s no denying it; Google will continue to dominate in this segment.

People were worried that searching through Gemini would reduce ad revenue for Alphabet because it doesn’t show ads; however, since Gemini’s much deeper integration into Google Search, like the AI mode currently, which integrates ads within AI-generated responses, the ad revenue has been growing at a reasonable rate. Just this past quarter, it grew over 12% y/y, which doesn’t sound like it is going the way of the dodo just yet. The company did a great job at integrating Gemini, so there are no cannibalization worries going forward in my view.

The Central Theme- Gemini Integration

Speaking of Gemini, it is easily the most promising product that the company has come up with recently. It is hard to pinpoint exactly what kind of revenues Alphabet is getting from its AI tool, Gemini, but if you dig a little deeper, you will see that Gemini encompasses all avenues of the company. I already mentioned that Gemini is enhancing revenues for Google Search. It also supports GOOG’s cloud AI services through Google Cloud’s Vertex AI platform for enterprises, helping them deploy AI applications. Gemini also created essentially a new revenue stream since it offers AI-powered premium subscriptions and API services, helping GOOG to diversify income even more away from ad revenue, which is still significant.

The company is not going to roll over in this race since we already know that they are increasing its capex spend for the rest of the year, and I don’t think Alphabet is going to release the gas pedal in 2026 either. With so much money being thrown around, Alphabet is looking to be the leader in AI, and to be honest, I could see this happening. The way the company has integrated Gemini in many different facets of business and personal life, I think, is the most comprehensive AI platform out there currently.

Amazon’s (AMZN) Q is strong in enterprise/cloud assistant, but not so much in the consumer search, Apple (AAPL) is slowly advancing, but many see it lagging quite a bit, including me, while Meta’s (META) LLaMA 2.0 is a powerful foundational tool, which is used mostly in research and AI applications outside of direct search service is neat. MSFT’s Copilot will give Gemini a run for its money, the most in my opinion, as Copilot is deeply integrated in workplace productivity and business automation, which, if that was the focus of my point, the win would go to Copilot, but it isn’t, so Gemini’s broader appeal to consumers and cloud platforms takes the win for now.

Financials

I already mentioned how impressive the company’s balance sheet is; what I haven’t mentioned yet is how impressive the company’s other metrics are, particularly its profitability ratios. The three metrics I like to look at are the ROA, ROE, and ROIC, all of which are well above what I look for.

The company’s ROIC is especially impressive. For such a return, I would be willing to pay a premium.

Additionally, the company’s free cash flow has been tremendous over the last while, with some drop off recently due to the capex spending, but it is still very impressive.

Overall, the company is in a very solid financial situation.

Reasons Not to Invest

Competition in AI is Fierce

So, looking back, the company’s Gemini wasn’t all the success it may seem right now. Back when OpenAI rocked the world in 2022 with ChatGPT, Alphabet scrambled to come up with a competitor, which turned out to be Gemini. The beginning was quite messy and didn’t look like it was made by such an impressive company as Alphabet. The AI competition remains fierce. Billions of dollars are being funneled into the advancement of AI chatbots and tools. With competitors like the ones I mentioned, OpenAI, and other tech giants, there is no clear winner yet, and if Alphabet fumbles again, it could be the end of the dream of being the leader in the space. All it takes is just one massive stumble, and you’re out of the race. I don’t see it happening, but it is always a possibility.

There are still reasons to believe that Google Search will lose its dominance eventually, especially if Gemini falls behind. One of the reasons the judge ruled in Alphabet’s favor recently is because of the ever-changing AI space competition. It has only been a few years since it all began, so the world is yet to see what is truly achievable. A new company might swoop in out of nowhere and become the leader. What most likely outcome will be is that it will be bought out by a big tech firm, and that acquisition will be the reason one of the big tech firms comes out on top.

Conflicting Valuations

I think the company has become a bit rich to add at these levels. Let me expand on this a bit more. I ran a few valuation models to see what a good entry point would be and whether I should add right now to my position. Below are my findings.

Firstly, I went ahead and did a 5-year DCF analysis of the company, with the following assumptions.

I decided not to increase or decrease the company's average sales growth, as I believe 15% is quite achievable given the investments for growth it has been making. Operating margins, I believe, will improve once the heavy capex spending period subsides. Same with capex as a % of Revenue, which is very elevated right now and will not be sustainable. For my discount rate, since the company's profitability ratios are very attractive, I went with its WACC of 8.3% instead of the usual 10, because I said I would be willing to pay a premium.

The result of these assumptions gave me the following trajectory of the company’s revenue and free cash flow projections.

With that said, the company’s intrinsic value, or fair value, is around $230 a share, which means it may be slightly overvalued.

I went ahead and did a comps analysis against the mentioned peers, which are not many, making the valuation a bit less meaningful. It is quite lonely up top here in the trillions of market cap. Nevertheless, if we compare GOOG to its peers, we can see that its fair value is higher than based on FCF estimates and is currently undervalued.

I used only suitable companies like AMZN, AAPL, MSFT, and Meta, as these are the ones that are similar in size and are involved in AI and other related fields. Mainly, I compared the following multiples (in the future, I plan to add more comparisons for a more comprehensive look). We can see that most of these companies trade slightly higher than GOOG on average. I think the comps analysis tells us a bit more about how every big tech is overvalued more than GOOG is undervalued, in my view.

From a P/E perspective, which values companies on earnings, we can see that GOOG is commanding the lowest multiple. EV/EBITDA measures enterprise profitability; here, we can see GOOG is somewhere in the middle. P/S compares revenues, with the largest outlier being AMZN, while GOOG is once again somewhere down the lower end. P/B measures asset valuation, while EV/Revenues measures the overall size of the business, and we can see that GOOG is consistently somewhere down the bottom.

Now, if we take each individual multiple, we can see that comparing GOOG by P/E ratio, it would be worth around $338, but in this instance, we are taking the median valuation of the multiples to give us a fair value of $303.06.

We can take an average of the two valuation outcomes to come to a fair value of around $266 a share, which I think seems reasonable.

Closing Comments

I think the company’s share price could be lower, and I would welcome a small crash here right now; however, I am confident that it is still better to buy a company at a fair value than to wait for the right time to get in. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating and added some more here for my long-term portfolio and will gladly pick up more if we see some profit-taking coming in over the next few quarters due to the company’s recent run-up.

I believe there is a lot to like in the long run regarding the company’s AI efforts and overall revenue situation. Google Search is going strong, as well as YouTube, posting similar revenue growth numbers, which shows that the dominance is here to stay. At least for now

data.title: Why The Correction Is Starting
data.description:
We haven't seen a lot of pain in the markets for a long time. March and April, especially, were intense months. However, the massive black-swan induced correction ultimately brought the market to one of its most remarkable buying opportunities in stock market history.

S&P 500 Chart

The S&P 500 (SP500) "SPX" dropped by a very significant 22% in about a six-week time frame. The level of panic observed during this tariff-shock period was unprecedented. The VIX surged to about 60, and the Fear and Greed Index hit its second lowest level on record, a 3.

Given that there wasn't a deadly pandemic ravaging the globe, and there wasn't much conclusive evidence of an imminent recession, the fundamental backdrop did not seem to warrant such incredible panic.

The VIX - "Fear Index"

The VIX surged like the global economy was coming to an end in early April. This dynamic highlighted that sentiment simply became much too negative, which is why I essentially went all-in on high quality stocks right as the VIX peaked. I increased many of my top tech positions in pre-market on April 7th, as the SPX was nearing 4,800 support.

The S&P 500 was down by about 5-6% in early pre-market that morning. Stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Dell (DELL), Palantir (PLTR), and many others were down by 10-20% just that morning. Some of the higher alpha momentum stocks were down even more.

Thus, I realized that this was very likely the pinnacle of the panic-driven capitulation bottom, and it was time to buy. After all, it's like the Oracle of Omaha, Warren Buffett says - "You buy when there is blood in the streets."

From Extreme Fear to Extreme Greed

We actually witnessed one of the most rapid rebounds in stock market history, with the fear and greed index surging from its second most fearful level on record to extreme greed in fewer than three months.

While the rally slowed down recently, the initial stages of it were breathtaking. The SPX is now about 40% above its April 7th bottom, and the Nasdaq 100 has skyrocketed by approximately 52%.

Now, these are returns you can proudly write home about. Still, I can't help but notice some negative near-term factors developing beneath the market, that may enable a more notable correction to materialize as we advance.

A Broader Correction Seems Likely

Gradually, I've gotten more cautious on some stocks since around mid-July. While I've remained mostly fully invested, I've taken some profits, and rotated quite a bit.

The Fear and Greed Index peaked in early July, which is when the "smart money" began increasing put option protection. We also saw momentum ETFs like Cathie Wood's ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) and others peak.

ARKK ETF Chart

However, this dynamic led only to transitory, rolling corrections, first in momentum stocks, and then in some industry specific tech, and other segments. However, we did not witness anything resembling a broad market correction, or a solid pullback even since this new bull run began almost six months ago now.

For instance, while ARKK pulled back by over 10% from mid to late July, the Nasdaq 100 only saw a 4.5% pullback in the last two days of July. The S&P 500's most significant decline since April's bottoming process was only 3.5% during the same two day period.

Meme Stocks Gone Wild

Now, we are seeing momentum stocks going wild again. I want to be clear, that this is not like late 2021, but I can't deny several obvious similarities. Just look at the ARKK ETF. It made another high recently, and is now about 120% higher from its April low. However, ARKK's appreciation is minuscule compared to some individual stocks.

Oklo: 10-Month Chart

Oklo (OKLO) may be the epitome of a momentum stock gone wild here. Now, don't get me wrong, I love Oklo. I was long this stock since the teens from late 2024, but now it is just getting ridiculous, in my view.

The stock has risen by about 750% from its April bottom, the RSI is around 90 here, and fundamentally, this company is nowhere near generating profitability. Moreover, its market cap is over $20 billion now, and it may not even show any revenues for several years.

I like Oklo's prospects, and it's been my go to SMR stock since I got into the nuclear space about a year ago. And this has been an incredibly lucrative and successful investment. However, paying up this much for a company that's trading at 12 times 2034 earnings estimates seems absurd to me. Who knows what kinds of issues Oklo could run into in the future? How much capital will it need to raise before anything considerable gets built? After all, building nuclear reactors is a very costly business. This is especially true when we're dealing with newer technologies. How much stock dilution will there be?

Meme Stocks Are Making A Big Comeback

Meme stocks are making a big comeback, and it's concerning to see, as it reminds me of the market top in 2021. Oklo is essentially a meme stock now, and of course, Oklo is not alone. I love Palantir. I built it into my most significant portfolio position back when the stock was $6-7. That's how much conviction I had in Palantir, when very few other people did.

Palantir: 2.5 Year Chart

It turned out to be a phenomenal investment, likely one of my top five stock investments of all time, especially given the very short time frame of the 2,500%+ move.

Still, I was uber-bullish and accumulating when very few people seemed to understand how remarkable Palantir's AI potential could be, and the stock was trading around six times forward sales.

Now, everyone seems to be discussing Palantir. My taxi driver told me I should buy back into Palantir at current prices, because he owns it, and it's going to be the next trillion dollar company, he said.

That's phenomenal I thought, and Palantir may very well become a trillion dollar company, in several years. However, we should remain realistic. Trees don't grow to the sky, and stocks shouldn't be shooting up in a straight line either.

Again, I love Palantir, I understand that it's essentially a monopoly, and it has an enormously long growth runway. Yet, it's trading at 75 times next year's sales estimates, and its forward P/E ratio is about 216 here.

Also, the company has a market cap of about $425 billion here, and should generate slightly over $4 billion in sales this year. This just doesn't seem reasonable to a growth/value investor like me. So, Palantir has become a meme stock and it's been one for a while now.

So, What Is A Meme Stock?

Having a meme stock status is not necessarily a bad thing, but it does raise some red flags, and not just about Palantir, but the market in general. When we speak of meme stocks, we typically think of GameStop (GME), or AMC Entertainment (AMC), and other such companies, which may be on the brink of bankruptcies, but are getting bid up by retail investors irrationally.

However, meme stocks can come in very different shapes and sizes. Essentially any company can become a meme stock if enough retail investors become overly excited about it and then push its valuations through the roof.

Valuations and the future potential of companies may be subjective. Therefore, some may consider Tesla (TSLA) a meme stock, despite its enormous potential in various highly lucrative futuristic industries.

I am well aware of this fact, despite Tesla being my most considerable portfolio holding now. I am mindful of a possible drawdown in the near term, which is why I took some profits recently, and hedged my Tesla position with some put options.

Many stocks in quantum computing, AI, nuclear, and other segments have extremely high multiples, and are overextended from a technical standpoint now as well.

This "Bubblicious" dynamic increases the probability for a correction or a more significant pullback, as the market may look for reasons or excuses even to take profits. Moreover, any disappointing news flow or data could impact sentiment negatively, which can often turn on a dime, leading to sharp increases in volatility and near term turbulence in markets.

Look, The Smart Money Is Getting More Cautious

Talk about extreme greed. Just look at the put to call option ratio here. This is actually by far the most critical component of the fear and greed index, in my view, and it's screaming caution now.

This may be one of the greediest moments in recent history, according to the put to call option spreads, as it hit an ultra-low level of just 0.57 a few days ago.

Now, we're witnessing a rebound, suggesting that the smart money is likely starting to increase put option protection, similarly to what we witnessed in early July. However, this dynamic does not mean that we won't see a more considerable broad market pullback or a correction this time.

Shiller P/E Ratio - Valuations Still Matter

There's no way to sugarcoat this. The Shiller P/E ratio is very high. In fact, it's higher than in 2021, and it's essentially climbing toward the obscenely high dot-com bubble era levels. Now, granted, the Shiller P/E ratio may not be as relevant as it has been in previous decades, but at the end of the day, valuations still matter.

One of the primary reasons why I am not calling for a bear market here as I did at the top in 2021, is because the Fed changed the game. Inflation was sky high, and the Fed was going into a tightening cycle, and there was an obvious slowdown approaching then.

This Time It Is Different - Why It's Not Like 2021

This time, inflation is reasonable, the Fed is reentering an easing cycle, and the economy may actually reaccelerate next year. Due to the Fed's ultra-low interest rate policies, QE, and other tactics, multiples can expand for longer than anticipated and could go higher than expected. But don't expect stocks to move up in a straight line.

Also, we can't just say that valuations don't matter, and that stocks can be bid up perpetually without any consequences. We already tried that in the 90s, and we all know how the dot-com bubble ended, not great for many market participants.

Therefore, we must be mindful of the excessive valuations in "meme stocks," and stocks in general. To avoid a significant market meltdown, we actually need a correction now. As much as people may fear transitory 5-10% drawdowns, they are necessary, and are a normal occurrence during any bull market phase.

Despite the possibility for some near term turbulence, and a potential pullback or a correction in the near-term, I remain highly constructive on the U.S. economy and markets in the intermediate and longer term. I am keeping my year-end SPX target range at 7,000-7,200.

data.title: AI's Real Winner Is Actually Micron, And It's Surprisingly Cheap
data.description:
When a megatrend as disruptive as AI enters the scene, it's important to look for players that are truly capable of structurally positioning themselves at the heart of the opportunity. To me, Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) represents exactly that kind of player.

I first initiated coverage of the company back in early May, when it traded at $80, attracted by the bottleneck economics it faced in the high bandwidth memory space. Since then, the stock's price has more than doubled, as the company has continued to demonstrate strong technological leadership in memory technologies and exceptional execution in terms of capturing the strong demand from data centers and hyperscale AI workloads.

The company recently reported its FY25 and Q4 results, showing strong growth capture and a structural boost in profitability. I highlight in this assessment that Micron, owing to its technological leadership in the space, is uniquely positioned to command both premium pricing and deep, long-term relationships with hyperscale clients. Broadly speaking, I see the company as being one of the most direct beneficiaries in the AI megatrend.

What's even more remarkable is that MU stock seems very attractively priced, given its strong outlook and its various multiples, relative to its peers.

As a result, I am reiterating my Strong Buy stance on the stock.

Financial Results

As a lot of us expected, Micron Technology came in with very solid financial results, beating both the top and bottom line estimates for Q4 FY25.

On a top line basis, the company reported a revenue total of $37.4 billion for the year, compared to the $25.1 billion total in 2024, which implies a nearly 50% jump. This was driven by record data center sales and a very sharp recovery across mobile, PC, and automotive segments.

Similarly, gross profit almost tripled in absolute terms, during the year, from $5.6 billion to $14.9 billion. What is even more outstanding is the fact that the gross profit margin expanded dramatically from 22.4% to 39.8%, in just 12 months. This, of course, is a very positive improvement in the company's value generation potential for its shareholders, driven by a more advantageous pricing environment, an improved product mix, and cost optimizations.

Operating income for FY25 surged to $9.8 billion, up from only $1.3 billion one year ago. As a result, net income for the year was reported at $8.5 billion, denoting a 650% annual jump from $778 million in 2024.

Broadly speaking, we see very clear signs that the company is entering a period of structural profitability anchored by AI adoption, given Micron's ongoing HBM and DDR5 ramps.
